source_textbook.name: Gilbert Strang: Introduction to Linear Algebra (18.06)
source_textbook.chapter: 5-7 Linear Transformations, Inner Product, Least Squares
source_textbook.section: 5.1-7.4 Linear Maps, Inner Products, Gram-Schmidt, Least Squares

extracted_concepts.name: linear transformation
extracted_concepts.definition_snippet: A map T: V → W preserving addition and scalar multiplication

extracted_concepts.name: inner product
extracted_concepts.definition_snippet: A bilinear, symmetric, positive-definite form ⟨u, v⟩

extracted_concepts.name: inner product space
extracted_concepts.definition_snippet: Vector space with an inner product defined

extracted_concepts.name: orthonormal basis
extracted_concepts.definition_snippet: Basis where all vectors are orthogonal and unit length

extracted_concepts.name: Gram-Schmidt
extracted_concepts.definition_snippet: Process to convert any basis to orthonormal basis

extracted_concepts.name: least squares
extracted_concepts.definition_snippet: Best approximate solution: x̂ = (A^T A)⁻¹ A^T b

extracted_concepts.name: orthogonal projection
extracted_concepts.definition_snippet: Nearest point in a subspace to a given vector

extracted_relations.evidence: Linear transformations act between vector spaces
Kernel captures the null space of a transformation
Image captures the range of a transformation
Inner product space extends vector space with inner product
Orthonormality is defined via inner product
Least squares solution is an orthogonal projection
Norm is derived from inner product
